slides.title: What an API is
body: API = Application Programming Interface. The contract one piece of software exposes for others to use.

Real example: every modern app talks to dozens of APIs. Your iPhone alone talks to Apple's iCloud API, Google Maps API, weather APIs, and Stripe whenever you Apple-Pay. Each is a contract: 'send me X, I'll give you Y back.'

vocabulary.def: A contract one program exposes for other programs to call — a defined set of inputs and outputs.

slides.title: REST in 60 seconds
body: REST = a style of API. Operations are HTTP verbs: GET (read), POST (create), PUT/PATCH (update), DELETE.
Real example: 'GET /accounts/123' fetches account 123. 'POST /charges' creates a Stripe charge. The verb tells the server what kind of action you want.

vocabulary.def: Representational State Transfer — the most common HTTP API style. Verbs (GET/POST/...) on URLs identify operations.
A specific URL on the API server that does one thing — like /accounts/123.

slides.title: Status codes you'll see
body: 200 OK · 201 Created · 204 No Content · 400 Bad Request · 401 Unauthorized · 403 Forbidden · 404 Not Found · 429 Rate Limited · 500 Server Error.
Real example: any time you've seen 'Error 404' on a webpage, that's the same status code an API would return. Same vocabulary, browser or program.

slides.title: JSON — the data format
body: Most modern APIs send and receive JSON. It's a text format with keys, values, lists, and nested objects — readable by humans and easy for any language to parse.
Example: { "name": "Acme", "contacts": [{ "email": "x@y.com" }] }

vocabulary.def: JavaScript Object Notation — text format used as the body of most modern API requests and responses.

slides.title: Authentication basics
body: API key (simplest), Bearer token (modern standard), OAuth 2.0 (for user-level permissions). Always sent in headers, never in URLs.
Real example: Stripe uses bearer tokens. Twilio uses basic auth. Salesforce uses OAuth 2.0. Every API documents which it expects.

vocabulary.def: Standard for user-permissioned authentication — user grants your app access without sharing their password.

slides.title: Apex callouts — the players
body: Three classes: Http (the dispatcher), HttpRequest (what you send), HttpResponse (what you get back).
Pattern: build a request, send it via Http().send(request), inspect the response.

slides.title: A simple GET callout
slides.intro: Type this. Fetch a public weather endpoint and inspect the response.
lineExplanations: Construct an HttpRequest. Empty so far.
Set the URL we're calling.
HTTP verb — GET to read.
Construct the dispatcher.
Send the request, capture the response.
Inspect the status code. 200 = success.

slides.title: Headers and JSON body
body: POST/PUT requests usually carry a JSON body. Set the Content-Type header to application/json. Authentication usually goes in an Authorization header.
Real example: Stripe's POST /charges expects { amount, currency, source } as JSON in the body, and your secret key as 'Authorization: Bearer sk_xxx'.

slides.title: POST with JSON body and auth
slides.intro: Type this. Send a JSON body with auth headers.
lineExplanations: Construct.
Endpoint URL.
POST method.
Bearer auth header.
Tell the server we're sending JSON.
Set the body — JSON string. In real code, build this with JSON.serialize() instead.
Send it. Construct Http inline to keep it tight.

slides.title: Parsing the JSON response
body: JSON.deserialize(jsonString, MyClass.class) maps a JSON string into an Apex class instance. JSON.deserializeUntyped returns a Map<String, Object> — useful when shape is unknown.
Real example: Stripe's response shape is documented; you build a matching Apex class and JSON.deserialize directly into it.

vocabulary.def: Convert a JSON string into a typed Apex object.

slides.title: Deserializing the body
slides.intro: Type this. Pull the response body into a typed Apex variable.
lineExplanations: JSON.deserializeUntyped returns Object; cast to Map<String, Object> to read keys.
Pull a value out by key, cast it to String. Same dot-syntax as any Apex Map.
Print the parsed id.

slides.title: Named Credentials
body: Stop hardcoding API URLs and secrets. Salesforce's Named Credentials store the URL + auth in admin config. Reference 'callout:MyService/path' instead of a literal URL.
Real example: every production-grade Apex integration uses Named Credentials. Rotating an API key becomes an admin-side change, not a code deploy.

vocabulary.def: Salesforce admin config storing endpoint URL and auth — referenced from Apex via 'callout:Name' so secrets stay out of code.

slides.title: Calling via Named Credential
slides.intro: Type this. The 'callout:Stripe' prefix replaces the literal URL — Salesforce handles auth automatically.
lineExplanations: Construct.
callout:Stripe references the Named Credential. Salesforce expands to the real URL and injects auth headers.
Verb.
Send. No hardcoded secret in this code.

slides.title: Exposing Apex as REST
body: @RestResource(urlMapping='/v1/leads/*') marks a class as a REST endpoint. @HttpGet and @HttpPost mark methods that handle each verb.
Real example: a partner system needs to push leads into your Salesforce org. Build a small @RestResource. Their POST hits your handler.

vocabulary.def: Annotation that exposes an Apex class as a public REST endpoint at a given URL.

slides.title: A REST endpoint
slides.intro: Type this. Anyone with proper auth can POST to /services/apexrest/v1/leads.
lineExplanations: @RestResource with the URL pattern. This class is now an HTTP endpoint.
Class must be global (visible across packages and clients).
@HttpGet marks the method that handles GET requests.
Handler — global static, returns whatever you want serialized.
Body. The framework auto-serializes the return as JSON.
Closes.

slides.title: Governor limits on callouts
body: 100 callouts per transaction. 120-second timeout per callout. Triggers can NEVER make callouts directly — must go async (covered in the Async Apex lesson).
Real example: forgetting the trigger-can't-callout rule is the #1 'why doesn't this work' question for new Apex devs.

slides.front: Why must callouts from triggers go through async (Queueable / @future)?
slides.back: Triggers run inside the database transaction; making a slow external call would block the database write. Salesforce blocks it.
slides.detail: Pattern: trigger collects ids → enqueues a Queueable → the Queueable runs the callout in its own transaction.

slides.question: You're hitting the same external API from many places. Where should the URL and API key live?
options: In a custom setting in plain text.
In a Named Credential — referenced as 'callout:Name'.
In a constant in a utility class.
slides.explanation: Named Credentials. Centralized, secrets never appear in code, rotating keys is an admin change.

slides.title: Capstone: Stripe charge POST via Named Credential
slides.intro: A customer just clicked 'Pay' in your storefront. Build the Apex callout that posts the charge to Stripe — using a Named Credential so the secret never lives in code, parsing the JSON response, and branching on the status code. The exact shape every payment integration uses.
lineExplanations: Build a fresh HttpRequest. Empty so far.
Endpoint via Named Credential — 'callout:Stripe' resolves to the real URL with auth attached. No secrets in code.
POST verb — we're creating a charge.
Stripe's charge endpoint expects form-encoded, not JSON.
Build the form-encoded body. amount is in cents (2500 = $25.00).
Attach the body to the request.
Send. Returns an HttpResponse holding status, headers, body.
Check status. 200 = OK, 4xx = client error, 5xx = server error.
Parse the JSON body into a generic map. Cast because deserializeUntyped returns Object.
Pull the charge id out of the parsed response. Cast back to String.
Log the successful charge.
Otherwise fall to the failure branch.
Log the status and body for diagnosis.
Close the else.

slides.title: What you now know
body: REST basics, status codes, JSON, callouts (Http/HttpRequest/HttpResponse), Named Credentials, exposing Apex as REST, callout governor limits.
Next: Caching — how to make APIs and queries fast and cheap by avoiding repeated work.
